provenance.source_corpus: Genetic and ecological assessment of population rehabilitation: walleye in Lake Superior

source_spans.text: the 2004 and 2005 releases of ﬁngerlings contributed 71 % and 45 % of the ﬁsh in their respective age classes.
the 2004 and 2005 releases of ﬁngerlings contributed 71 % and 45 % of the ﬁsh in their respective age classes.
the 2003 release of fry had no measurable contribution
the 2003 release of fry had no measurable contribution
there was little genetic evidence of interbreeding or natural recruitment of stocked ﬁsh.
there was little genetic evidence of interbreeding or natural recruitment of stocked ﬁsh.
In 1960 a logging dam was completed 17 km from the mouth of the Black Sturgeon River
which isolated Black Bay walleye from 80 % of the spawning habitat historically available to these ﬁsh
walleye stocked as ﬁngerlings have much higher survival than walleye stocked as fry
walleye stocked as ﬁngerlings have much higher survival than walleye stocked as fry
Removing the Black Sturgeon River dam would thus restore habitat connectivity and contribute to the recovery of the natural walleye population in Black Bay.
Removing the Black Sturgeon River dam would thus restore habitat connectivity and contribute to the recovery of the natural walleye population in Black Bay.
Historical overexploitation and habitat loss have both been recognized as important contributing factors to this collapse
Historical overexploitation and habitat loss have both been recognized as important contributing factors to this collapse
Historical overexploitation and habitat loss have both been recognized as important contributing factors to this collapse
rainbow smelt predation has previously been linked to declines in walleye recruitment
rainbow smelt predation has previously been linked to declines in walleye recruitment
Improvements in the abundance of walleye have occurred independently of hatchery stocking, with 75% of the current walleye population in Black Bay having a wild origin.
Improvements in the abundance of walleye have occurred independently of hatchery stocking, with 75% of the current walleye population in Black Bay having a wild origin.
